title: Twin Cam 96 cam chain tensioner — improved but not eliminated
description: HD upgraded to hydraulic tensioners with metal shoes on 2007+ TC96 models, but they still wear. The metal shoes last longer than TC88 plastic, but high-mileage bikes (50K+) still need inspection. Early 2007-2009 models may have been retrofitted with older-style plastic shoes from dealer inventory.
causes: Metal tensioner shoe wear at high mileage
Early models may have plastic shoes
fix_procedure: 1. Pull cam cover and inspect at 50K miles or if ticking develops. 2. Check for shoe wear marks. 3. If worn, upgrade to gear-driven cams or replace tensioners. 4. Forum tip: TC96 tensioners last 3-4x longer than TC88 plastic ones, but they're not permanent. At 50K+, inspect. Many shops still recommend gear-drive conversion at the 50K service as insurance.

title: ABS module failure — ABS light on, intermittent ABS activation
description: 2008+ Touring and some Dyna models with ABS can experience ABS module failure. The ABS light stays on, or worse, ABS activates randomly during normal braking. The module is expensive and requires programming to the bike's VIN. Common failure in harsh weather climates.
fix_procedure: 1. Scan for ABS codes first — could be a sensor, not the module. 2. Check wheel speed sensors for debris/damage — clean with brake cleaner. 3. Inspect ABS connector at the module for corrosion. 4. If module is confirmed bad, replace and program to VIN (dealer or aftermarket programmer). 5. Forum tip: before buying a $700 ABS module, check the wheel speed sensor gap and clean both sensors. 70% of ABS lights are sensor issues, not module failures. Also check the tone ring (mounted on the wheel hub) for damaged teeth — a single missing tooth triggers ABS codes.
parts_needed: Brake fluid (bleed after any ABS work)

title: ECM tuning — factory lean condition and decel popping
description: Factory ECM calibration on TC96/103 models runs lean for emissions compliance. Causes decel popping (backfire sound on closed throttle), hot running, and surging at cruise. Every TC96+ owner eventually gets a tuner. This isn't a 'failure' — it's a factory compromise that tuning fixes.
fix_procedure: 1. If stock exhaust and air cleaner: install a fuel management module (FP3, Power Vision, ThunderMax). 2. If aftermarket exhaust: a tune is REQUIRED — running an open exhaust on stock tune will lean out and damage the engine. 3. Dyno tune is best, but a canned map from your exhaust manufacturer is acceptable. 4. Forum tip: the Vance & Hines FP3 is the most user-friendly option — connects via Bluetooth phone app. ThunderMax is the gold standard but costs more and requires laptop. For a budget option, a Dobeck TFI module adds fuel without reprogramming the ECM.
parts_needed: EFI tuner (FP3, Power Vision, or ThunderMax)
Wideband O2 sensor kit (for dyno tune)

title: 6-speed transmission — hard shift into 5th or false neutral
description: The Cruise Drive 6-speed transmission in TC96+ models can develop hard shifting into 5th gear or a 'false neutral' between 4th and 5th. The shift forks wear and the shift drum detent spring weakens over time. More common on touring models with luggage and passenger.
fix_procedure: 1. First: change transmission fluid to fresh Spectro or RedLine heavy shockproof. 2. If still hard shifting, adjust clutch cable free play. 3. If false neutral persists, transmission needs to come apart — shift forks and/or shift drum. 4. Forum tip: Baker Drivetrain makes a shift kit that improves shift feel significantly. Also, many false neutral issues are actually clutch drag — make sure the clutch is fully releasing before tearing into the transmission.
parts_needed: Transmission fluid (Spectro or RedLine Heavy Shockproof)
Baker shift kit (if doing trans work)

title: Stator failure — same Harley story, different decade
description: TC96+ stators continue the Harley tradition of charging system failures. The 50-amp stator in later models is more robust but still fails from heat. CAN bus-era bikes show P0562 on the dash.
fix_procedure: 1. Read codes — P0562 confirms low system voltage. 2. Test stator AC output per leg at 2000 RPM (spec: 20-28V AC for 50-amp). 3. Check for shorts to ground. 4. Replace stator and regulator together. 5. Forum tip: same advice as every Harley — Cycle Electric kit is the upgrade. Also, ALWAYS disconnect the battery before removing the stator connector, and use dielectric grease on reconnection.

title: Throttle-by-wire (TBW) throttle body failure — 2008+ models
description: 2008+ models switched to throttle-by-wire (electronic throttle). The throttle body motor or position sensor can fail, causing limp mode, erratic idle, or no-throttle-response conditions. The bike goes into reduced power mode and limits RPM.
fix_procedure: 1. Scan codes — P0120/P0122/P0123 point to TPS circuit, P1510 means limp mode active. 2. Check throttle body connector for corrosion. 3. Test TPS voltage with key on, engine off — should sweep smoothly 0.5V to 4.5V. 4. If motor is bad, replace entire throttle body (motor is not separately serviceable). 5. Forum tip: before replacing the throttle body ($500+), check the wiring harness where it routes past the steering neck — the wires flex with every turn and can break internally while looking fine externally. Also try a TPS relearn procedure first (key on, full throttle, hold 10 sec, key off).
parts_needed: Wiring harness repair kit (if wire break found)

title: Compensator noise — worse on 103/110 CVO models
description: The compensator issue continues on TC96+ models, but is worse on the 103ci and 110ci CVO engines due to higher torque. The increased torque loads wear the compensator spring pack faster.
fix_procedure: 1. Same diagnosis as TC88 — clunk on startup, rattle at idle transitions. 2. SE Screamin Eagle compensator (40117-08A) or Baker Buddy. 3. Forum tip: on 110ci CVO models, the Baker Buddy is strongly preferred — the stock SE compensator still develops play faster on the 110 due to torque. Budget $250-300 for the Baker Buddy vs $150 for the SE compensator, but the Baker lasts indefinitely.

title: Oil cooler lines leaking — Touring models
description: Touring models with oil coolers (standard on 2009+) can develop leaks at the oil cooler line fittings. The rubber hose sections deteriorate from heat and oil exposure. A slow drip becomes a steady leak if ignored.
fix_procedure: 1. Inspect oil cooler lines — look for wetness at fittings and along hose length. 2. Replace hose sections if cracked or swollen. 3. Use new O-rings at fittings. 4. Forum tip: Jagg oil cooler kit is a popular upgrade if the stock cooler is leaking — better cooling and uses braided stainless lines that last longer. Don't overtighten the fittings — they seal on O-rings, not torque.
parts_needed: Oil cooler hoses (OEM or Jagg braided stainless)

title: Wheel bearing failure — Touring and Dyna
description: Wheel bearings on TC96-era bikes, especially Touring models with heavy loads, wear out around 30-50K miles. Symptoms include a humming noise that changes with speed (not RPM), and play felt when rocking the wheel side-to-side with the axle installed.
causes: Bearing wear from mileage and loading
fix_procedure: 1. Jack up wheel and spin — listen for roughness, feel for play. 2. Grab top and bottom of tire and rock — any play = bearing shot. 3. Press out old bearings, press in new (requires bearing press or puller). 4. Replace both bearings in the wheel (they come in pairs). 5. Replace wheel spacers if grooved. 6. Forum tip: use All Balls Racing bearing kits — come with bearings, seals, and spacers for about half the price of OEM. Grease the seals with waterproof marine grease before installation for longer life.

title: Fuel pump failure — no start, no prime sound
description: The in-tank fuel pump on EFI TC96+ models can fail, especially on bikes that run the tank low frequently (fuel cools the pump). On key-on, you should hear the pump prime for 2 seconds — if no prime sound, pump is suspect.
fix_procedure: 1. Key on — listen for 2-second pump prime whirr from the tank. No sound = pump or relay. 2. Check fuel pump fuse and relay first (cheap and easy). 3. Check voltage at pump connector — should see 12V during prime. 4. If voltage present but no prime, pump is dead — replace. 5. Forum tip: when replacing the fuel pump, also replace the fuel filter/screen inside the tank — it's a $10 part that's almost always clogged on a failed pump. Never run the tank below 1/4 — the fuel cools the pump, and running dry overheats and kills it faster.
